name: Mock Test - 01
instruction: This question paper contains three sections - A, B and C. Each part is compulsory. Section - A has 20 MCQs, attempt any 16 out of 20. Section - B has 20 MCQs, attempt any 16 out of 20 Section - C has 10 MCQs, attempt any 8 out of 10. There is no negative marking. All questions carry equal marks.

questions.question_text: The range of f(x) = x {tex}+\frac{1}{x} \text { is }{/tex}
questions.question_html: The range of f(x) = x {tex}+\frac{1}{x} \text { is }{/tex}
questions.explanation: f(x) = x + {tex}\frac{1}{x}{/tex} Range of the function can be given by putting values of x and find y. x -3 -2 -1 1 2 3 y -3.33 -2.5 -2 2 2.5 3.33 graph for the above function is It is clear from the graph that the Range of the function is {tex}(-\infty,-2] \cup[2, \infty),\{y | y \leq-2, y \geq 2\}{/tex}

questions.question_text: Minimize Z = 50x+60y , subject to constraints x +2 y ≤ 50 , x + y ≥ 30, x, y ≥ 0.
questions.question_html: Minimize Z = 50x+60y , subject to constraints x +2 y ≤ 50 , x + y ≥ 30, x, y ≥ 0.
questions.explanation: Here , Maximize Z = 50x+60y , subject to constraints x +2 y ≤ 50 , x + y ≥ 30, x, y ≥ 0. Corner points Z = 50x +60 y P( 50 ,0 ) 2500 Q(0 , 30) 1800 R( 10, 20 ) 1700 Hence the minimum value is 1700

questions.question_text: Let f(x) = |sin x| Then
questions.question_html: Let f(x) = |sin x| Then

options.text: f is everywhere continuous but not differentiable at x = (2n + 1) {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} , x {tex}\in{/tex} Z
options.html: f is everywhere continuous but not differentiable at x = (2n + 1){tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}, x {tex}\in{/tex} Z

options.text: f is everywhere continuous but not differentiable at {tex}x=n \pi, n \in \mathbf{Z}{/tex}
options.html: f is everywhere continuous but not differentiable at {tex}x=n \pi, n \in \mathbf{Z}{/tex}

options.text: f is everywhere differentiable x = (2n - 1) {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} , x {tex}\neq{/tex} Z

questions.correct_option_text: f is everywhere continuous but not differentiable at {tex}x=n \pi, n \in \mathbf{Z}{/tex}
questions.explanation: We have, {tex}f(x)=|\sin x|{/tex} Let f(x) = vou(x) = v[u(x)] [where, u(x) = sin x and v(x) = |x|] = v(sin x) = |sin x| Where, u(x) and v(x) are both continuous. Hence, f(x) = vou(x) is also a continuous function but v(x) is not differentiable at x = 0 So, f(x) is not differentiable where sin x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = n {tex}\pi{/tex} , n {tex}\in{/tex} Z Hence, f(x) is continuous everywhere but not differentiable at x = n {tex}\pi{/tex} , n {tex}\in{/tex} Z

questions.question_text: The number of solutions of the system of equations: 2x + y - z = 7, x - 3y + 2z = 1, x + 4y - 3z = 5, is
questions.question_html:
The number of solutions of the system of equations:
2x + y - z = 7,
x - 3y + 2z = 1, 
x + 4y - 3z = 5, is
questions.explanation: The given system of equations is : - 2x + y – z = 7 x – 3y + 2z = 1 x + 4y – 3z = 5 The matrix equation corresponding to the above system is {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & 1 & -1 \\ 1 & -3 & 2 \\ 1 & 4 & -3 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} \mathrm{X} \\ \mathrm{y} \\ \mathrm{z} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 7 \\ 1 \\ 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Let A = {tex}{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & 1 & -1 \\ 1 & -3 & 2 \\ 1 & 4 & -3 \end{array}\right]{/tex} and B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 7 \\ 1 \\ 5 \end{array}\right] {/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}|\mathrm{A}|=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & 1 & -1 \\ 1 & -3 & 2 \\ 1 & 4 & -3 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = 2(9 - 8) - 1( -3 - 2) - 1(4 + 3) = 2 + 5 - 7 = 0 and adjA = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 5 & -7 \\ -1 & -5 & -7 \\ 1 & -5 & 7 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} (adjA)B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 5 & -7 \\ -1 & -5 & -7 \\ 1 & -5 & 7 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} 7 \\ 1 \\ 5 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} -23 \\ -47 \\ 37 \end{array}\right] \neq 0{/tex}

questions.question_text: The feasible region for a LPP is shown in Figure. Evaluate Z = 4x + y at each of the corner points of this region. Find the minimum value of Z, if it exists
questions.question_html: The feasible region for a LPP is shown in Figure. Evaluate Z = 4x + y at each of the corner points of this region. Find the minimum value of Z, if it exists
questions.explanation: Corner points Z = 4x + y (0, 2 ) 2 (0,3) 3 (2,1) 9 Hence the minimum value is 2

questions.question_text: Maximum value of x + cos x in {tex} \left[ 0,\frac { \pi }{ 2 } \right]{/tex} is
questions.question_html: Maximum value of x + cos x in {tex} \left[ 0,\frac { \pi }{ 2 } \right]{/tex} is
questions.explanation: f(x) = x + cosx f'(x) = 1 - sinx For maximum and minimum values of f(x) , we have f'(x) = 0 Now, f'(x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow 1–sinx=0\Rightarrow x=\frac { \pi }{ 2 } {/tex} Hence, maximum value of f(x) is {tex}f(\frac { \pi }{ 2 } )=\frac { \pi }{ 2 } {/tex}

questions.question_text: If A is an invertible matrix of order 3, then which of the following information is NOT true?
questions.question_html: If A is an invertible matrix of order 3, then which of the following information is NOT true?

options.text: (AB) -1 = B -1 A -1 , where B = [b ij ] 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 and |B| {tex}\ne{/tex} 0
If BA = CA, then B {tex}\ne{/tex} C, where B and C are square matrices of order 3
options.html: If BA = CA, then B {tex}\ne{/tex} C, where B and C are square matrices of order 3

questions.correct_option_text: If BA = CA, then B {tex}\ne{/tex} C, where B and C are square matrices of order 3
questions.explanation: BA = CA {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} BAA -1 = CAA -1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} BI = CI {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} B = C

questions.question_text: The radius of a sphere is increasing at the rate of 0.2 cm/sec. The rate at which the volume of the sphere increases when radius is 15 cm, is
questions.question_html: The radius of a sphere is increasing at the rate of 0.2 cm/sec. The rate at which the volume of the sphere increases when radius is 15 cm, is
questions.explanation: {tex}V=\frac{4}{3} \pi r^{3}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d t}=4 \pi r^{2} \frac{d r}{d t}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d t}=4 \pi(15)^{2} \times 0.2=180 \pi \mathrm{cm}^{3} / \mathrm{sec}{/tex}

questions.question_text: In a LPP, the objective function is always
questions.question_html: In a LPP, the objective function is always
questions.explanation: In an LPP, the objective function is always linear. This is because these problems are always subjected to linear inequalities, where we maximise or minimise the linear functions.

questions.question_text: A square matrix A is called idempotent if
questions.question_html: A square matrix A is called idempotent if
questions.explanation: If the product of any square matrix with itself is the matrix itself, then the matrix is called Idempotent.

questions.question_text: If y = a + bx 2 , a, b arbitrary constants, then
questions.question_html: If y = a + bx2, a, b arbitrary constants, then
questions.explanation: y = a + bx 2 {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 2bx {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = 2b {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{1}{x} \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}

questions.question_text: The value of objective function is maximum under linear constraints
questions.question_html: The value of objective function is maximum under linear constraints

options.text: at the centre of feasible region
options.html: at the centre of feasible region

options.text: the vertex which is maximum distance from (0, 0)
options.html: the vertex which is maximum distance from (0, 0)

options.text: at any vertex of feasible region
options.html: at any vertex of feasible region

questions.correct_option_text: at any vertex of feasible region
questions.explanation: In linear programming problem we substitute the coordinates of vertices of feasible region in the objective function and then we obtain the maximum or minimum value. Therefore, the value of objective function is maximum under linear constraints at any vertex of feasible region.

questions.question_text: The equation of the normal to the curve y = x + sin x cos x at {tex}x=\frac {\pi}{2}{/tex} is
questions.question_html: The equation of the normal to the curve y = x + sin x cos x at {tex}x=\frac {\pi}{2}{/tex} is
questions.explanation: y = x + sinx cosx {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=1-\sin ^{2} x+\cos ^{2} x{/tex} Slope of the tangent at {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} is 0. Slope of the normal is {tex}\frac{-1}{0}{/tex} At {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{2} \Rightarrow y=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Equation of normal, {tex}y-\frac{\pi}{2}=\frac{-1}{0}\left(x-\frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2 x=\pi{/tex}

questions.question_text: The function {tex}f(x) = {e^{|x|}}{/tex}
questions.question_html: The function {tex}f(x) = {e^{|x|}}{/tex}
questions.explanation: We have, f(x) = e |x| = {tex}\begin{cases}e^x, & x \geq 0 \\ e^{-x}, & x<0\end{cases} {/tex} LHD = {tex}\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(0-h)-f(0)}{-h}{/tex} = {tex}\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{e^{-(0-h)}-e^0}{-h}{/tex} = {tex}\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{e^h-1}{-h} {/tex} = -1 and RHD = {tex}\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(0+h)-f(0)}{h}{/tex} = {tex}\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{e^{0+h}-f(0)}{h}{/tex} = {tex}\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{e^h-1}{h}{/tex} = 1 {tex}\therefore{/tex} LHD {tex}\neq{/tex} RHD So, f(x) is not differentiable at x = 0. Hence, f(x) is differentiable everywhere except at x = 0.

questions.question_text: If k be an integer, then {tex}\mathop {Lt}\limits_{x \to k} \;\;{/tex} (x –[x])
questions.question_html: If k be an integer, then {tex}\mathop {Lt}\limits_{x \to k} \;\;{/tex} (x –[x])
questions.explanation: does not exists

questions.question_text: The point on the curve y 2 = x where tangent makes 45° angle with x-axis is
questions.question_html: The point on the curve y2 = x where tangent makes 45° angle with x-axis is
questions.explanation: y 2 = x differentiating with respect to x, {tex}2 y \frac{d y}{d x}=1{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{2 y}{/tex} Given that tangent makes an angle 45 o . {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\tan 45^{\circ}=1=m{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 1=\frac{1}{2 y}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} and x = y 2 {tex}\Rightarrow x=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex} Point is {tex}\left(\frac{1}{4}, \frac{1}{2}\right){/tex}

questions.question_text: The curves x= y 2 and xy = k cut orthogonally when
questions.question_html: The curves x= y2 and xy = k cut orthogonally when
questions.explanation: Let {tex}(\alpha,\beta){/tex} be the point of intersection of the given curves Now, {tex}x={ y }^{ 2 }\Rightarrow 2y\frac { { dy } }{ { dx } } =1\Rightarrow \frac { dy }{ dx } =\frac { 1 }{ 2y } .....(i){/tex} {tex}xy=k\Rightarrow x.\frac { dy }{ dx } +y=0\Rightarrow \frac { dy }{ dx } =\frac { -y }{ x } ...(ii){/tex} {tex} { m }_{ 1 }=\quad { \left( { \frac { { dy } }{ { dx } } } \right) _{ \left( { \alpha ,\beta } \right) } }={ \left( { \frac { { 1 } }{ { 2y } } } \right) _{ \left( { \alpha ,\beta } \right) } }=\frac { { 1 } }{ { 2\beta } }\ ,{ m }_{ 2 }=\quad { \left( { \frac { { dy } }{ { dx } } } \right) _{ \left( { \alpha ,\beta } \right) } }={ \left( { \frac { { -y } }{ { x } } } \right) _{ \left( { \alpha ,\beta } \right) } }=\frac { { -\beta } }{ { \alpha } }{/tex} Two curves cut orthogonallly means m 1 .m 2 = -1 {tex} \Rightarrow \frac { { 1 } }{ { 2\beta } } .\frac { { -\beta } }{ { \alpha } } =-1\Rightarrow 2\alpha =1\Rightarrow \alpha =\frac { 1 }{ 2 } ....(iii){/tex} Since {tex}{ \left( { \alpha ,\beta } \right) }\quad lies\quad on\quad x={ y }^{ 2 }\quad we\quad have\quad \alpha ={ \beta }^{ 2 }\Rightarrow { \beta }^{ 2 }=\frac { 1 }{ 2 } ....(iv){/tex} Also, Since, {tex}{ \left( { \alpha ,\beta } \right) }\quad lies\quad on\quad xy=k,\quad we\quad get\quad \alpha .\beta =k\Rightarrow { { k }^{ 2 }={ \alpha }^{ 2 }.\beta }^{ 2 }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow { { k }^{ 2 }=\frac { 1 }{ 4 } .\frac { 1 }{ 2 } =\frac { 1 }{ 8 } }\quad \quad \quad from\quad (iii)\quad and\quad (iv){/tex} {tex}\\ \Rightarrow 8{ k }^{ 2 }=1{/tex}

questions.question_text: The principal value of the expression cos –1 [cos (– 680°)] is
questions.question_html: The principal value of the expression cos–1 [cos (– 680°)] is
questions.explanation: cos –1 (cos (680°)) = cos –1 [cos (720° – 40°)] = cos –1 [cos (– 40°)] = cos –1 [cos (40°)] = 40° = {tex}\frac{2 \pi}{9}{/tex} .

questions.question_text: If xy - log e y = 1 satisfies the equation {tex}x\left(y y_{2}+y_{1}^{2}\right)-y_{2}+\lambda y y_{1}=0{/tex} , then {tex}\lambda{/tex} =
questions.question_html: If xy - loge y = 1 satisfies the equation {tex}x\left(y y_{2}+y_{1}^{2}\right)-y_{2}+\lambda y y_{1}=0{/tex}, then {tex}\lambda{/tex} =
questions.explanation: xy - log e y = 1 Differentiating both sides we get {tex}x y_{1}+y-\frac{y_{1}}{y}=0{/tex} xyy 1 + y 2 - y 1 = 0 y(xy 1 + y) - y 1 = 0 y(xy 2 + y 1 + y 1 ) + (xy 1 + y)y 1 - y 2 = 0 xyy 2 + 2yy 1 + x {tex}y_1^2{/tex} + yy 1 - y 2 = 0 x(yy 2 + {tex}y_1^2{/tex} ) - y 2 + 3yy 1 = 0 {tex}\therefore {/tex} the value of {tex}\lambda{/tex} is 3

questions.question_text: If y = x x then {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}={/tex} ?
questions.question_html: If y = xx then {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}={/tex} ?
questions.explanation: Given ,y = f(x) = x x Taking log both sides, we obtain log e y = x {tex}\times{/tex} log x(-1) (Since log a b c = c log a b) Differentiating (i) with respect to x, we obtain {tex}\frac{1}{y} \frac{d y}{d x}=x \times \frac{1}{x}+\log _{e} x \times 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=y \times\left(1+\log _{e} x\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=f^{\prime}(x){/tex} = x x (1 + log e x)

questions.question_text: Let S be the set of all real numbers and let R be a relation on S, defined by a Rb {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} |a - b| < 1. Then, R is
questions.question_html: Let S be the set of all real numbers and let R be a relation on S, defined by a Rb {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} |a - b| < 1. Then, R is

options.text: Symmetric and transitive but not reflexive
options.html: Symmetric and transitive but not reflexive

options.text: Reflexive and transitive but not symmetric
options.html: Reflexive and transitive but not symmetric

options.text: Reflexive and symmetric but not transitive
options.html: Reflexive and symmetric but not transitive

questions.correct_option_text: Reflexive and symmetric but not transitive
questions.explanation: Reflexivity: clearly for every a {tex}\in{/tex} S (real number) {tex}|a-a|=0<1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} R is Reflecxive Symmetric: (a, b) {tex}\in{/tex} R {tex}\Rightarrow \quad|a-b|<1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad|b-1|<1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow (b, a) \in R{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} R is Symmetric Transitive: (a, b), (b, c) {tex}in{/tex} R {tex}\Rightarrow | a - b| < 1{/tex} and {tex}|b-c|<1{/tex} Now, {tex}|a-c|=|a-b+b-c|{/tex} {tex}\leq |a-b|+|b-c|{/tex} Which need not be less than 1 So, R is not Transitive {tex}\therefore{/tex} R is Reflexive and symmetric but not transitive.

questions.question_text: The corner points of the feasible region determined by the system of linear constraints are (0, 10), (5, 5), (15, 15), (0, 20). Let Z = px + qy, where p, q > 0. Condition on p and q so that the maximum of Z occurs at both the points (15, 15) and (0, 20) is
questions.question_html: The corner points of the feasible region determined by the system of linear constraints are (0, 10), (5, 5), (15, 15), (0, 20). Let Z = px + qy, where p, q > 0. Condition on p and q so that the maximum of Z occurs at both the points (15, 15) and (0, 20) is
questions.explanation: Since Z occurs maximum at (15, 15) and (0, 20), therefore, 15p + 15q = 0p + 20q {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} q = 3p.

questions.question_text: The point on the curve y = 6x - x 2 at which the tangent to the curve is inclined at {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} to the line x + y = 0 is
questions.question_html: The point on the curve y = 6x - x2 at which the tangent to the curve is inclined at {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} to the line x + y = 0 is
questions.explanation: y = 6x - x 2 {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=6-2 x=m{/tex} also, x + y = 0 {tex}1+\frac{d y}{d x}=0{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=-1{/tex} Given that angle between the lines is {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} . {tex}\tan \frac{\pi}{4}=\left|\frac{6-2 x+1}{1-6+2 x}\right|{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 1=\left|\frac{-2 x+7}{-5+2 x}\right|{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{-2 x+7}{-5+2 x}=\pm 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 3 and put this in y = 6x - x 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 9 Point is (3, 9)

questions.question_text: The range of tan -1 x is
questions.question_html: The range of  tan-1x is
questions.explanation: The domain range of tan function is {tex}(- \frac{\pi }{2}\;,\;\frac{\pi }{2}\;){/tex} and R. But, tan function is one-one and onto only when we shrink range to (-1,1). Now tan function which is one and onto has domain {tex}(- \frac{\pi }{2}\;,\;\frac{\pi }{2}\;){/tex} and range (-1,1) Hence, domain and range of {tex}{\tan ^{ - 1}}{/tex} function is (-1,1 ) to {tex}(- \frac{\pi }{2}\;,\;\frac{\pi }{2}\;){/tex} .

questions.question_text: If sin -1 x - cos -1 x {tex}=\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex} , then x =
questions.question_html: If sin-1 x - cos-1 x {tex}=\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex}, then x =
questions.explanation: sin -1 - cos -1 x {tex}=\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}-\cos ^{-1} x-\cos ^{-1} x=\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}-2 \cos ^{-1} x=\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}-\frac{\pi}{6}=2 \cos ^{-1} x{/tex} {tex}\frac{2 \pi}{6}=2 \cos ^{-1} x{/tex} {tex}\frac{\pi}{3} \times \frac{1}{2}=\cos ^{-1} x{/tex} {tex}\frac{\pi}{6}=\cos ^{-1} x{/tex} {tex}x=\cos \frac{\pi}{6}{/tex} {tex}x=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}{/tex}

questions.question_text: The total revenue in Rupees received from the sale of x units of a product is given by R(x) = 3x 2 + 36x + 5. The marginal revenue, when x = 15 is
questions.question_html:
The total revenue in Rupees received from the sale of x units of a product is given by
R(x) = 3x2 + 36x + 5. The marginal revenue, when x = 15 is
questions.explanation: Marginal revenue (MR) is the rate of change of total revenue with respect to the number of units sold. So, MR = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{R}}{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}}{/tex} = 6x + 36 = 6x + 36 {tex}\therefore{/tex} when x = 15, then MR = 6(15) + 36 = 126 Therefore, the marginal revenue when x = 15 is 126.

questions.question_text: Let A be a square matrix of order 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 3, then | kA| is equal to
questions.question_html: Let A be a square matrix of order 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 3, then | kA| is equal to
questions.explanation: Let A be any 3×3 matrix {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{lll} {a} & {b} & {c} \\ {d} & {e} & {f} \\ {g} & {h} & {i} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore|\mathrm{A}|=\left|\begin{array}{lll} {\mathrm{a}} & {\mathrm{b}} & {\mathrm{c}} \\ {\mathrm{d}} & {\mathrm{e}} & {\mathrm{f}} \\ {\mathrm{g}} & {\mathrm{h}} & {\mathrm{i}} \end{array}\right|{/tex} Clearly, {tex} \mathrm{k} \mathrm{A}=\left[\begin{array}{lll} {\mathrm{ka}} & {\mathrm{kb}} & {\mathrm{kc}} \\ {\mathrm{kd}} & {\mathrm{ke}} & {\mathrm{kf}} \\ {\mathrm{kg}} & {\mathrm{kh}} & {\mathrm{ki}} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow|\mathrm{k} \mathrm{A}|=\left|\begin{array}{lll} {\mathrm{ka}} & {\mathrm{kb}} & {\mathrm{kc}} \\ {\mathrm{kd}} & {\mathrm{ke}} & {\mathrm{kf}} \\ {\mathrm{kg}} & {\mathrm{kh}} & {\mathrm{ki}} \end{array}\right|{/tex} Taking out k from the 1 st , 2 nd and 3 rd row, we get {tex}|\mathrm{k} \mathrm{A}|=\mathrm{k}^{3}\left|\begin{array}{lll} {\mathrm{a}} & {\mathrm{b}} & {\mathrm{c}} \\ {\mathrm{d}} & {\mathrm{e}} & {\mathrm{f}} \\ {\mathrm{g}} & {\mathrm{h}} & {\mathrm{i}} \end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} |kA| = k 3 |A| Therefore, answer is option (C) k 3 |A|

questions.question_text: The function, f (x) = (x – a) sin {tex}\frac{1}{{x - a}}{/tex} for x {tex} \ne {/tex} a and f (a) = 0 is
questions.question_html: The function, f (x) = (x – a) sin {tex}\frac{1}{{x - a}}{/tex}for x{tex} \ne {/tex} a and f (a) = 0 is

options.text: Continuous but not derivable at x = a
options.html: Continuous but not derivable at x = a

questions.correct_option_text: Continuous but not derivable at x = a
questions.explanation: {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to a} (x - a)\sin \frac{1}{{(x - a)}} {/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} h\sin \frac{1}{h} = 0 = f(a){/tex}

questions.question_text: If {tex}\sqrt x + \sqrt y = \sqrt {a,} {/tex} then {tex}{\left( {\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right)_{x = a}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.question_html: If {tex}\sqrt x + \sqrt y = \sqrt {a,} {/tex} then {tex}{\left( {\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right)_{x = a}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation: {tex}\sqrt x + \sqrt y = \sqrt a ........(1){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{{2\sqrt x }} + \frac{1}{{2\sqrt y }}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = - \frac{{\sqrt y }}{{\sqrt x }}.......(2){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} = - \frac{{\sqrt x \frac{1}{2}{y^{ - \frac{1}{2}}}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} - \sqrt y \frac{1}{2}{x^{ - \frac{1}{2}}}}}{x}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{ - \left( {\frac{{\sqrt x }}{{2\sqrt y }}\left( { - \frac{{\sqrt y }}{{\sqrt x }}} \right) - \frac{{\sqrt y }}{{2\sqrt x }}} \right)}}{x}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{\sqrt x + \sqrt y }}{{2x\sqrt x }} = \frac{{\sqrt a }}{{2x\sqrt x .}} = \frac{{\sqrt a }}{{2a\sqrt a }} = \frac{1}{{2a}}{/tex}

questions.question_text: If y = sin -1 {tex}\left\{\frac{\sqrt{1+x}+\sqrt{1-x}}{2}\right\}{/tex} then {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}={/tex} ?
questions.question_html: If y = sin-1 {tex}\left\{\frac{\sqrt{1+x}+\sqrt{1-x}}{2}\right\}{/tex} then {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}={/tex} ?
questions.explanation: Put x = cos 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} ,we get {tex}\Rightarrow y=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{1+\cos 2 \theta}}{2}\right.{/tex} {tex}\left.+\frac{\sqrt{1-\cos 2 \theta}}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{2 \cos ^{2} 2 \theta}}{2}+\frac{\sqrt{2 \cdot \sin ^{2} \theta}}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{\cos 2 \theta}{\sqrt{2}}+\frac{\sin 2 \theta}{\sqrt{2}}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y=\sin ^{-1}\left(\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{4}+2 \theta\right)\right.{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y=\frac{\pi}{4}+2 \theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d \theta}=2{/tex} Put {tex}\theta=\frac{\cos ^{-1} x}{2}{/tex} ,we get {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d} \theta}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{-1}{4 \sqrt{1-\mathrm{x}^{2}}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{-1}{2 \sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{/tex}

questions.question_text: Corner points of the feasible region determined by the system of linear constraints are (0, 3), (1, 1) and (3, 0). Let Z = px+qy, where p, q > 0. Condition on p and q so that the minimum of Z occurs at (3, 0) and (1, 1) is
questions.question_html: Corner points of the feasible region determined by the system of linear constraints are (0, 3), (1, 1) and (3, 0). Let Z = px+qy, where p, q > 0. Condition on p and q so that the minimum of Z occurs at (3, 0) and (1, 1) is
questions.explanation: We have Z = px + qy , At ( 3, 0 ) Z = 3p ……………………………….(1) At ( 1 , 1) Z = p + q …………………………(2) Therefore , from (1) and (2) : We have : p = q/2 .

questions.question_text: Let X = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} x_{1} \\ x_{2} \\ x_{3} \end{array}\right]{/tex} , A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rrr} 1 & -1 & 2 \\ 2 & 0 & 1 \\ 3 & 2 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} and B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 3 \\ 1 \\ 4 \end{array}\right]{/tex} . If AX = B, then X is equal to
questions.question_html: Let X = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} x_{1} \\ x_{2} \\ x_{3} \end{array}\right]{/tex}, A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rrr} 1 & -1 & 2 \\ 2 & 0 & 1 \\ 3 & 2 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} and B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 3 \\ 1 \\ 4 \end{array}\right]{/tex}.  If AX = B, then X is equal to
questions.explanation: Given that X = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} x_{1} \\ x_{2} \\ x_{3} \end{array}\right]{/tex} , A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & -1 & 2 \\ 2 & 0 & 1 \\ 3 & 2 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} and B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 3 \\ 1 \\ 4 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Also AX = B and we have to find the value of X , Pre-multiplying A -1 both sides we get, A -1 AX = A -1 B IX = A -1 B ( {tex}\because{/tex} A -1 A = I) X = A -1 B ( {tex}\because{/tex} IX = X) …(i) Now, {tex}|\mathrm{A}|=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & -1 & 2 \\ 2 & 0 & 1 \\ 3 & 2 & 1 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = 1(0 - 2) + 1(2 - 3) +2(4 - 0) = -2 - 1 + 8 = 5 And adjA = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} -2 & 5 & -1 \\ 1 & -5 & 3 \\ 4 & -5 & 2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} \mathrm{X}_{1} \\ \mathrm{X}_{2} \\ \mathrm{X}_{3} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} -1 \\ 2 \\ 3 \end{array}\right]{/tex} On comparing both sides we get, x 1 = -1, x 2 = 2 and x 3 = 3.

questions.question_text: Let {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{lr} 1, & x \leq-1 \\ |x|, & -1<x<1 \\ 0, & x \geq 1 \end{array}\right.{/tex} . Then, f is
questions.question_html: Let {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{lr} 1, & x \leq-1 \\ |x|, & -1<x<1 \\ 0, & x \geq 1 \end{array}\right.{/tex}. Then, f is
questions.explanation: Given that {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{aligned} 1, & x \leq-1 \\ |x|, &-1<x<1 \\ 0, & x \geq 1 \end{aligned}\right\}{/tex} Checking the continuity at x = -1: LHL at x =-1, {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow-1^{-}} f(x)=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} f(-1-h)=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} 1=1{/tex} RHL at x =-1, {tex}\lim\limits _{x \rightarrow-1^{+}} f(x)=\lim\limits _{h \rightarrow 0} f(1+h)=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0}(1+h)=1{/tex} Hence, f(x) is continuous at x =-1. Checking the differentiability at x =-1: LHD at x =-1, {tex}\lim\limits _{x \rightarrow-1^{-}} \frac{f(x)-f(-1)}{x-(-1)}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(-1-h)-f(-1)}{-1-h-(-1)}{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{1-1}{-h}=0{/tex} RHD at x =-1, {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow-1^{+}} \frac{f(x)-f(-1)}{x-1}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(-1+h)-f(-1)}{(-1)+h-(-1)}{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{1-1}{h}=0{/tex} {tex}\because{/tex} LHD = RHD

questions.question_text: R = {(1, 1), (2, 2), (1, 2), (2, 1), (2, 3)} be a relation on A, then R is
questions.explanation: A relation R on a non empty set A is said to be reflexive if xRx for all x {tex} \in {/tex} R , Therefore , R is not reflexive. A relation R on a non empty set A is said to be symmetric if xRy {tex} \Leftrightarrow {/tex} yRx, for all x , y {tex} \in {/tex} R. Therefore, R is not symmetric. A relation R on a non empty set A is said to be antisymmetric if xRy and yRx {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} x = y , for all x , y {tex} \in {/tex} R. Therefore, R is not antisymmetric.

questions.question_text: If {tex}2{\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {{\text{cos x}}} \right){/tex} = {tex}{\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {2\;cosecx} \right){/tex} , then x =
questions.question_html: If {tex}2{\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {{\text{cos x}}} \right){/tex} = {tex}{\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {2\;cosecx} \right){/tex}, then x =
questions.explanation: {tex}2{\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {\cos x} \right)\, = {\tan ^{ - 1}}2\left( {\cos ecx} \right){/tex} {tex}\\{\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{2\cos x}}{{1 - {{\cos }^2}x}}} \right)\,\, = \,{\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{2}{{sinx}}} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,2{\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( x \right)\,\, = {\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{2x}}{{1 - {x^2}}}} \right)\\{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{2\cos x}}{{{{\sin }^2}x}} = \frac{2}{{sinx}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \cot x = cot(\frac{\pi}{4})\,\,\, \Rightarrow x = \frac{\pi }{4}{/tex}

questions.question_text: In linear programming problems the function whose maxima or minima are to be found is called
questions.question_html: In linear programming problems the function whose maxima or minima are to be found is called
questions.explanation: In linear programming problems the function whose maxima or minima are to be found is called Objective function . A linear function which has to be maximised or minimised is called as the linear objective function.

questions.question_text: The differential coefficient of f(log x) with respect to x, where f(x) = log x is
questions.question_html: The differential coefficient of f(log x) with respect to x, where f(x) = log x is
questions.explanation: We have, f(x) = log x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(log x) = log(log x) {tex}\Rightarrow f^{\prime}(\log x)=\frac{1}{\log x} \frac{d}{d x}(\log x){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow f^{\prime}(\log x)=\frac{1}{x \log x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(log x) = (x log x) -1

questions.explanation: Let X = A - A’ X’ = (A - A’)’ = A’ - (A’)’ =A’ - A = -(A - A’) = -X Therefore (A - A’) is skew symmetric matrix.
